items.q: Why are cash flow and profit different on different time-scales?
items.correct: Profit is recognised when a sale is made (even on credit); cash flow is when money actually moves. A profitable sale today may produce no cash for 60 days while bills are due — creating a cash-flow problem despite profit.
items.misconception: Cash flow and profit are the same thing measured differently.
items.random_wrong: Cash flow only matters at year-end.
items.why: The timing gap between recognition and receipt is what sinks profitable companies. Students conflate the two accounting concepts, missing why working capital management is critical.

items.q: What is the difference between profit and revenue?
items.correct: Revenue is total income from sales (price x quantity sold); profit is revenue minus total costs — what is left after paying for everything.
items.misconception: Profit and revenue are different words for the same thing.
items.random_wrong: Profit is what businesses earn; revenue is what employees earn.
items.why: A business can have huge revenue but make no profit if costs are equally huge. Conflating them leads to nonsensical analysis — Amazon had high revenue but low profits for years.

items.q: Why can a profitable business still fail?
items.correct: Because cash flow and profit are different — a business may be profitable on paper but run out of cash to pay bills, especially if customers owe money or stock ties up funds.
items.misconception: If a business is profitable it cannot run out of money.
items.random_wrong: Profitable businesses fail only when employees steal.
items.why: Cash-flow problems sink profitable companies routinely. Profit is accounting; cash is liquidity. Students treat the two as equivalent and miss why businesses go bust despite paper profits.

items.q: Which is best for funding a SHORT-TERM cash shortage?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Bank overdraft. “Mortgage” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Issuing new shares mainly causes which DRAWBACK for existing owners?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Dilution of control. “Higher interest payments” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Revenue £200,000. Gross profit £80,000. What is gross profit margin, in %?
items.why: The real figure is 40. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
